meta.title: Object vs Map for keyed JavaScript collections
meta.summary: A dashboard keeps adding, removing, counting, and iterating items by id. Would you store that data in a plain object or a Map?

scenario: You are building a JavaScript dashboard that tracks selected rows by id. The UI needs to add and remove entries often, check whether an id already exists, show a count badge, iterate through the current selections, and clear the whole collection quickly. The ids are string values today, but the set behaves like a living collection rather than a fixed record shape. In the interview, the panel asks whether you would store this in a plain object or a Map.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Use a plain object
options.summary: Fits well when the data behaves like a simple record with predictable string keys and a JSON-like shape.
whenItWins: The structure is really a plain record, config object, or API-shaped dictionary keyed by known string names.
You mainly want straightforward property access and easy JSON serialization without a collection-specific API.
The data is not being treated like a heavily mutated collection with lots of insert, delete, count, and iteration work.
watchOutFor: Once the value starts acting like a dynamic collection, you often end up rebuilding collection helpers yourself with Object.keys, manual counts, and delete operations.
A weak answer picks object just because it is more familiar, without asking whether the data is actually a record or a collection.

options.label: Use a Map
options.summary: Fits well when the value behaves like a dynamic keyed collection that you will add to, remove from, count, and iterate over frequently.
whenItWins: The data acts like a live collection with repeated set, get, has, delete, clear, size, and iteration operations.
You want the code to communicate intent clearly: this is not just an object shape, it is a collection with lifecycle operations.
The collection may evolve over time, and you want a structure designed for keyed entries instead of borrowing plain objects for that job.
watchOutFor: If the value really needs to stay a plain JSON-shaped record, Map can add unnecessary conversion and mental overhead.
A weak answer says Map is always better without checking whether the data is actually just a simple record with string keys.

decisionMatrix.title: Plain record or config shape
decisionMatrix.prompt: The value mostly behaves like a simple object of named fields or a JSON-style dictionary.

cells.note: This is easier to defend when the data is naturally record-shaped and does not need collection-style operations.
It can still work, but you now need to explain why a collection API is worth it for a shape that is mostly just a plain record.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: Entries are added, removed, counted, iterated, and cleared like a living keyed collection.

cells.note: Possible, but the code often starts collecting helper work that Map already models directly.
This is easiest to defend because the data behaves like a collection and Map expresses that intent clearly.

decisionMatrix.title: Data must cross a plain JSON boundary
decisionMatrix.prompt: The value is frequently sent over the network or stored in a format that naturally expects plain objects.

cells.note: This becomes easier to defend when plain serialization and record shape matter more than collection ergonomics.
Still viable internally, but now you should explain the conversion cost and why the collection API is still worth it.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is this data really a record or really a collection?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first question. Plain records and dynamic collections often look similar at first, but they age differently in real code.

evaluationDimensions.title: Which operations happen most often?
evaluationDimensions.description: Frequent add, remove, clear, count, and iteration work usually pushes you toward a collection-oriented structure.

evaluationDimensions.title: What does the code communicate to the next developer?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer considers whether the chosen structure makes it obvious that the value is a plain record or a live keyed collection.

evaluationDimensions.title: Does the data need to stay plain JSON-friendly?
evaluationDimensions.description: Sometimes the best structure is shaped by what the rest of the system expects to receive, store, or serialize.

evaluationDimensions.title: Are you rebuilding collection behavior by hand?
evaluationDimensions.description: If your object solution keeps needing extra helper logic for size, iteration, or clearing, that is usually a sign the data may want a collection structure.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean Map because the prompt describes a dynamic collection, not just a plain record
strongAnswer.summary: This dashboard state is being added to, removed from, checked, counted, iterated, and cleared as a live keyed collection. That makes Map easier to defend for the exact prompt.
reasoning: If this were a plain config object or a JSON-shaped dictionary of known fields, I would be happy to keep it as an object because that shape is simpler and more natural there.
In this prompt, the important clue is not only the keys. It is the operation pattern: has, set, delete, clear, size, and iteration are central to the problem.
Map communicates that collection intent more clearly and avoids turning a plain object into a do-it-yourself collection API.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would use Map for the live keyed collection and only move back toward a plain object if the data later needs to behave more like a simple serialized record than a dynamic collection.

interviewerPushback.question: Can't an object do all of this too?
interviewerPushback.answer: It can, but that is not the same as being the clearest model. Once the state behaves like a collection with add, delete, count, clear, and iteration as first-class operations, Map usually expresses the intent better and avoids extra helper code.

interviewerPushback.question: When would you still prefer an object?
interviewerPushback.answer: When the value is really a plain record or JSON-like dictionary and the code mainly wants simple property access or serialization instead of collection operations.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the data stopped behaving like a live collection and instead became a simple API-shaped object that mostly gets read and serialized as plain key-value data, I would move back toward an object.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use Map because it is more advanced than object.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds like tool worship and ignores the actual shape and operations in the prompt.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use Map because there will be add and delete operations.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a clearer explanation of why the data behaves like a collection and when object is still the better fit.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would use Map because the state behaves like a live keyed collection: I need to add entries, remove them, test membership, iterate them, count them, and clear them. If the value were really just a plain JSON-style record with predictable string keys, I would keep it as an object. Here the collection behavior is the deciding signal, not just the fact that keys exist.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it starts from the operation pattern, explains why Map matches the intent, and still gives a clean boundary for when object remains the simpler choice.

answerFramework: Start by naming whether the value behaves like a plain record or a live keyed collection.
List the main operations the code must support in this prompt.
Choose the structure that matches those operations most naturally.
Acknowledge when the other option is still the better answer, especially around JSON-shaped data.
End with one clear boundary, for example: object for records, Map for dynamic keyed collections.
antiPatterns: Choosing Map only because it feels more advanced or modern.
Choosing object only because it is more familiar, even when the code is rebuilding collection behavior by hand.
Ignoring serialization and record-shaped boundaries when plain objects are still the more natural fit.
Talking only about keys instead of talking about operations and intent.
